topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification hit my screen like a physical blow. A formal accusation, citing evidence of copied text. My hands, which had been steady moments before, started to tremble uncontrollably. I reread the email three times, the words blurring slightly. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward until my chest felt constricted, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. The fluorescent lights of the library seemed too bright, casting harsh shadows that made the room feel suddenly suffocating. I wanted to flee, to disappear, but my feet felt rooted to the polished floor.

[story 2]

"You need to sit down, Alex." Professor Davies’ voice was level, but the quiet authority in it sent a tremor through me. Every muscle in my jaw clenched. I stared down at the stack of papers on my desk, the ink seeming to mock me with its undeniable similarity to the submitted work. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim momentarily. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat alarming. "I… I don't understand," I managed, my voice coming out thin and reedy. My gaze darted around the lecture hall, searching for an escape route, any opening to retreat from this sudden, overwhelming pressure.

[story 3]

The summons arrived tucked inside a thick manila envelope. When I finally opened it, the contents—the formal complaint—felt heavy, dense, pressing down on my shoulders. My breath hitched. I placed the paper on the table, and my fingers began to pick at the edge of the desk blotter, a nervous, repetitive motion. My heart was hammering against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm I couldn't silence. I felt a flush creep up my neck, hot and undeniable. Looking at the accusing signature, the world seemed to tilt slightly off its axis. I squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying sense of precariousness.
